config.title: Water quality: A foundation for health and development
config.abstract: Access to safe drinking water is essential for health and development, yet over two billion people, mostly in low- and middle-income countries, still lack it. Many remain exposed to contaminated water, which threatens health, livelihoods, and economic progress, underscoring the urgent need to ensure not only access, but also water safety.
config.citation: Kashiwase, Haruna and Tony Fujs. 2026. “Water quality: A foundation for health and development”.  In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A F Pirlea, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, D Wadhwa and M Welch. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
config.inputs: Christian Borja-Vega, Pavel Luengas Sierra, Fan Zhang
config.homeFactoidText: countries, water quality is the main constraint in attaining universal access to safely managed drinking water
config.homeTagline: Water quality remains the main barrier to safely managed drinking water

content.text: Poor water quality is a global concern

Reliable access to safely managed drinking water (clean, safe and available at home) is essential for health and well-being. While such access is often taken for granted in high-income countries, 2.1 billion people—about a quarter of the world’s population— still rely on water sources that may be far away, irregular, or contaminated.

content.title: People without access to safely managed drinking water
content.label: In 2024, globally

content.text: Poor water quality has significant human and economic costs. Contaminated drinking water is a major driver of preventable disease. It poses serious health risks, especially for infants and young children, with potential long-term consequences for health and cognitive development.[reference: chemical_risks] These risks are global and affect all regions, including high-income countries.[reference: ecoli_wy] That is why achieving universal access to safely managed drinking water is a target (6.1.1.) under the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals.
What is considered safe drinking water?

Evaluating the quality of drinking water involves looking at both [emphasis: water sources ]( the type of infrastructure providing water) and [emphasis: water service levels  (]how well these sources provide water).

A Joint Monitoring Programme for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene (JMP) developed by the WHO/UNICEF  classifies water sources as improved or unimproved. [emphasis: Improved sources] are designed to reduce the risk of contamination, and include pipes, tube wells, and protected wells or springs. [emphasis: Unimproved sources] include unprotected wells, springs, and surface water (rivers, lakes, and ponds). But while improved water sources are likely to be protected from outside contamination, this does not ensure safe water quality; nor does it consider accessibility or availability.

To encapsulate a holistic perspective of safe drinking water, JMP classifies drinking water services into five levels: [emphasis: safely managed], [emphasis: basic], [emphasis: limited], [emphasis: unimproved], and [emphasis: surface water].[reference: water_levels]

content.title: A fourth of the global population lacks safe drinking water
content.subtitle: Five levels of drinking water services (% of population)
content.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SH.H2O.SMDW.ZS)
content.visdescription: Line chart showing the distribution of the global population across drinking water service levels from 2000 to 2024. Each line represents a service level—safely managed, basic, limited, unimproved, and surface water—and together they sum to 100 percent in each year. The chart illustrates how the composition of service levels has changed over time.
content.keyFactLabel: People lacked access to safely managed drinking water in 2024.
content.yAxisTitle: Access to drinking water, by service level

scenes.text: [emphasis: safely managed]: The highest service level, this is water from an[emphasis: improved source] that is [emphasis: available on premises],[emphasis: when needed, ]and [emphasis: free from fecal and priority chemical contamination such as arsenic and fluoride]. Global access rose from 61 percent (3.8 billion people) in 2000 to 74 percent (6 billion) in 2024. Still 2.1 billion people remain without safe drinking water.

[emphasis: basic][emphasis: ][emphasis: : from an improved source, often off-premises but within a 30-minute round trip and without guaranteed water quality. ]In 2024, about 18 percent of the world’s population (1.4 billion people) had access to this level of drinking water.

In 2024, a tenth of the global population even lacked basic drinking water: while 4 percent had access to an improved source but with a collection time of over 30 minutes (referred as [emphasis: limited] services), another 4 percent relied on [emphasis: unimproved ] sources. Finally, 1 percent were at high risk of contamination who depended on [emphasis: surface] water accessing it directly from rivers, dams, lakes.

content.text: Although global access to safely managed drinking water has improved, significant disparities remain, with rural dwellers lagging their urban counterparts.

content.title: Access to safely managed drinking water remains much lower in rural areas
content.subtitle: People using safely managed drinking water services, by residence (% of population)
content.visdescription: Two line charts showing the distribution of the global population across drinking water service levels from 2000 to 2024, one for urban areas and one for rural areas. In the first scene, each line represents a service level—safely managed, basic, limited, unimproved, and surface water—and together they sum to 100 percent in each year. In the next scene, line charts are shown for eight regions, including the world. For each region, two line charts—urban and rural—show trends in safely managed drinking water access over the same period. In the final scene, South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa are highlighted.
content.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SH.H2O.SMDW.RU.ZS, SH.H2O.SMDW.UR.ZS).
content.note: Rural population data are unavailable for North America (2000–24) and East Asia & Pacific (2000–14).

scenes.text: While the proportion of urban dwellers with [emphasis: safely managed] drinking water stagnated at just over 80 percent between 2000 and 2024, the share of rural dwellers increased substantially, from about 40 percent to 60 percent over the same period.
Despite this, across all regions with available data, rural access still lags urban access by about 20 percentage points.

South Asia is an exception: the urban–rural gap narrowed sharply between 2000 and 2024 as access expanded in rural areas. Sub-Saharan Africa has also seen gains in rural access, but coverage remains extremely low, at 17 percent in 2024. Low access to water services is often concentrated in poorer regions, where limited public resources and the high cost of extending infrastructure to dispersed rural populations constrain progress, leaving these populations particularly vulnerable. [reference: water_poverty]

content.text: Next, we examine the overall levels of access to safely managed drinking water at country level.

content.title: Access to safely managed drinking water is low in Sub-Saharan Africa and small island economies in the Pacific
content.subtitle: People using safely managed drinking water services, 2024 (% of population)
content.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SH.H2O.SMDW.ZS).
content.note: The 69 countries in the gray diagonal hatching have no available estimates of safely managed drinking water access in 2024.
content.visdescription: Map showing the share of population using safely managed drinking water services, by country. Countries are shaded according to their level of access, with darker colors indicating higher coverage and lighter colors, lower coverage. Countries with no color indicate missing data or no available estimates.
content.legendLabel: Access to safely managed drinking water (% of population)

scenes.text: Data on access to safely managed drinking water services are available for 148 countries for 2024. Due to insufficient water monitoring or testing systems, approximately a third of countries still lack water quality data.
Among the countries with data available, only 29 countries have achieved universal (99 percent or higher) access to safely managed drinking water.

Low access is concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa. In 23 of 26 Sub-Saharan Africa countries with data , fewer than half of their populations have access to safe drinking water. At approximately 6 percent, Central African Republic and Chad have the lowest access to safe drinking water.

content.text: How is access to safely managed drinking water estimated?

Because the data for the required components often come from different sources and surveys, they cannot always be measured for the same population. To avoid overstating access, the methodology uses a conservative approach, taking the [emphasis: lowest ]value among the three components as the estimate of safely managed drinking water access. The component with the lowest value is also referred to as the[emphasis: limiting factor to access.]

content.title: Safely managed drinking water access is determined by the lowest value of the three component
content.subtitle: People with access to each component: water free from contamination, or water accessible on premises, or water available when needed (% of people using improved water sources)
content.source: WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene, JMP Global Database.
content.visdescription: Three column charts show the components of safely managed drinking water among people using improved water sources. The left column represents water that is free from contamination; the middle column,  water that is accessible on premises; and the right column, water that is available when needed. The first scene shows data for  Mongolia;  the second scene shows data from Nepal.

scenes.text: For example, [emphasis: in Mongolia], 84 percent of individuals with improved water sources have available water when needed, and 82 percent access uncontaminated water. But [emphasis: only 43 percent ]have improved water sources at home, resulting in an estimates of [emphasis: 43 percent ]of people with safely managed drinking water access. Thus, for Mongolia, accessibility is the limiting factor.

Conversely, [emphasis: in Nepal], accessibility and availability are relatively high (around 80 percent each), but [emphasis: only 16 percent have access to ][emphasis: uncontaminated water.] As a result, only around [emphasis: 1][emphasis: 6 percent] have access to safely managed drinking water. So, in Nepal, free from contamination is the limiting factor in access to safely managed drinking water.

content.text: Water quality is the biggest challenge

Across 94 countries with available data,[footnote:  Excludes 29 countries with universal access (99%+) and 25 countries lacking availability data (54 in total). Safely managed drinking water access is estimated when all three components are available, or when water quality data are available with at least one other component.] [emphasis: water quality is the most common limiting factor] —in 48 countries (just over half of all countries)— followed by accessibility, and availability, in 23 countries (about a quarter of the countries) each.

content.title: Countries face different challenges in achieving access to safely managed drinking water
content.subtitle: People with access to each component: water free from contamination, or water accessible on premises, or water available when needed (% of people using improved water sources)
content.source: WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene, JMP Global Database.
content.visdescription: Three column charts showing the components of safely managed drinking water among people using improved water sources. The left column represents water that is free from contamination, the middle column, water that is accessible on premises, and the right column, water that is available when needed. In the final scene, a grid map shows countries as equally sized tiles, each displaying level of access to safely managed drinking water and its three components: accessibility, availability, and water quality. Within each tile, bars represent the level of each component, allowing comparison of limiting factor across countries.
content.keyFactLabel: of countries (48 out of 94) with data face water quality challenges, which is the most common constraint among the three components of safely managed drinking water (accessibility, availability, and quality).

scenes.text: In several Pacific Island nations, including Tuvalu, Nauru, and Tonga, the share of people with [emphasis: water free from contamination] is extremely low while the share of people with water readily available and accessible on premises is less of a concern. As many small atoll islands have no rivers or large lakes, most of their drinking water comes from rainwater harvesting, and these systems are easily contaminated or diluted by saltwater, while water treatment capacity is limited.[reference: waterq1][reference: waterq2] Some Southeast Asian countries also face significant water quality challenges including Cambodia, Indonesia, and Lao People’s Democratic Republic where inadequate wastewater treatment contributes to contamination. [reference: UNEP]

[emphasis: Accessibility] is a major challenge in many Sub-Saharan African countries, such as Democratic Republic of the Congo, Malawi, Guinea-Bissau, Lesotho and São Tomé and Príncipe, where access to water on premises remains limited. These countries often lack adequate infrastructure,[reference: wateracc1] particularly in rural areas where communities are widely dispersed.[reference: wateracc2]

Water is considered available when needed if households report having sufficient water or access to it most of the day[footnote: For example, at least 12 hours per day or 4 days per week, depending on the survey.]. In countries such as South Africa and Algeria, chronic water scarcity can disrupt supply systems. However, availability depends not only on water scarcity but also on infrastructure and water management. Some water-scarce countries maintain reliable access through desalination or well-managed distribution systems.[ref: ZAF][reference: DZA][reference: CCC]

The map presents the limiting factors for safely managed drinking water access in 94 countries. Please note that gray diagonal hatching indicates countries that have attained universal access, countries with safely managed drinking water data but no data for availability, or those with no data for safely managed drinking water data.

content.text: As well as being the most common limiting factor across countries, progress in improving water quality has also lagged the other two components of safely managed drinking water. While many countries have expanded physical access to water and improved availability, ensuring that water is free from contamination remains a challenge. Tanzania illustrates this pattern clearly.

content.title: Progress in improving water quality lags behind progress in availability and accessibility
content.subtitle: People using improved water sources, by service component and years left to reach universal access (% of population)
content.x_axis_title_typical: Years left to reach universal access
content.source: WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene, JMP Global Database; World Bank staff calculations.
content.note: For details on the progress speed, refer to the Explainer and Explore Data sections.
content.visdescription: Three line charts showing the share of population meeting each component of safely managed drinking water— quality, accessibility, and availability—from 2000 to 2024. Each line represents one component, and the slope indicates how quickly it is changing.  In the final two scenes, the charts  compare progress in Tanzania with the typical trajectory.

scenes.text: In 2000, while nearly 15 percent of people in Tanzania had access to uncontaminated water, only 1 percent of Tanzanians had access to water on premises.

Over the next two decades, accessibility improved dramatically but water quality lagged. Infrastructure expanded rapidly and by 2024, almost half the population had water on premises. Yet only about one-third had access to uncontaminated water.

Is this pattern unique to Tanzania? Let’s take a  look at the average path of progress across countries from 2000 to 2024. Using historical country-level data, we estimate a “typical” trajectory of improvement for each component of safely managed drinking water (see [progress-paper] for the methodology).

The contrast is striking. On the typical path, increasing water accessibility would take just over 30 years, increasing coverage from 40 percent of the population to 75 percent — would take less than 36 years along the typical path.
Achieving the same scale of progress in water quality would take nearly 100 years, or three times longer.

content.text: Improving water quality is often more challenging than improving accessibility and availability because these can often be achieved through one-time infrastructure investments. By contrast, maintaining water quality requires continuous management of pollution sources and sustained monitoring, so progress is slower and more complex.[reference: who_guideline][reference: wb_water_quality][reference: unesco_water_quality]

Water quality can also be compromised by human activities, at household and large-scale agricultural and industrial levels. Sewage and other wastes are often discharged into rivers, lakes, and wetlands, allowing fecal contamination to spread through interconnected water systems, especially when wastewater is untreated. Untreated human excreta is one of the most significant sources of contamination, closely linked to the lack of safely managed sanitation.[reference: link_san][reference: link_bacteria]
E.coli contamination
The presence of the bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) is the key measure of fecal contamination of drinking water.

Water contaminated by human feces can lead to acute diseases and can pose life-threatening risks, particularly to infants and young children who are most vulnerable as their immune systems are still developing.
Every year, half a million people die from diarrheal diseases due to drinking unsafe water. More than half (54 percent) are children younger than five years. [reference: burden_disease]

To assess fecal contamination through household surveys, the presence of E. coli is measured in a 100 milliliter (mL) water sample. According to WHO guidelines, water is classified as low risk if it contains fewer than 1 E. coli per 100 mL, moderate risk if it contains 1–10, high risk if it contains 11–100, and very high risk if it exceeds 100 E. coli per 100 mL.

content.title: Risk levels of fecal contamination
content.subtitle: E.coli colony-forming units (CFU)/100mL
content.source: WHO, UNICEF, World Bank. State of the world’s drinking water. Geneva: World Health Organization; 2022.
content.note: CFU measures the number of viable E. coli bacteria in a water sample, expressed as CFU per 100 mL.
content.visdescription: Table showing E. coli risk levels based on the number of CFU per 100 mL of water. Risk levels are classified as low (fewer than 1 CFU per 100 mL), moderate (1 to 10), high (11 to 100), and very high (more than 100).

content.text: Water can not only be contaminated when collected, it can get further contaminated during transport or storage—for example through unhygienic or uncovered containers, dirty ladles or cups, or contaminated hands.[reference: ecoli_storage]

In household surveys such as the Multiple Indicator Cluster Surveys (MICS), water quality testing is conducted both at the point of collection, that is [emphasis: at the household’s primary drinking water source] (such as , tap, well, or protected spring) and at point of use, that is [emphasis: within the household]—typically from a drinking glass or container. The water is tested for E. coli at both points to assess the level of contamination.

content.title: E. coli contamination at point of use is worse than at point of water collection
content.subtitle: 30 countries with the largest shares of population using drinking water sources with high or very high fecal contamination risk (% of population)
content.source: Progress on household drinking water,sanitation and hygiene 2000–2024: special focus on inequalities. Geneva: World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF), 2025.
content.visdescription: Dumbbell chart comparing E. coli contamination levels measured at point of collection and at point of use. For each country, two values are shown to indicate contamination at source and after water is stored or handled in households. The comparison illustrates differences between these two stages and highlights how contamination can change between collection and use.
content.keyFactLabel: more than half the population is exposed to high or very high E. coli contamination risk in drinking water.

scenes.text: Let’s look at the shares of people who use water with high and very high E. coli risk levels at[emphasis: ][emphasis: point of collection]. In 15 countries, at least half of the population collects drinking water that already has high or very high levels of E. coli contamination.

Contamination rates are even higher when measured at [emphasis: point of use]. In 26 out of 30 countries with data, more than half the population drinks water with high or very high risk of E.coli contamination, as shown by household-level tests.

In 28 out of the 30 countries, contamination levels [emphasis: at ][emphasis: point of use] were higher than at [emphasis: point of collection]. For example, in [emphasis: Malawi], 36 percent of the population are exposed to high or very high levels of contamination at point of collection, rosing to 81 percent at point of use.

content.text: In many countries with available data, households store water prior to consumption, which can increase the risk of contamination.

As a result, household water treatment—such as boiling, chlorination, filtration, or solar disinfection—is an important public health intervention to reduce bacterial and other microbial contamination. But treatment practices are often uncommon, and when reported, they are not always effective due to incorrect or inconsistent application.[reference: ecoli_research]
E.coli contamination: urban-rural gap

Urban–rural disparities in water contamination are widespread. In most countries with available survey data, rural populations face substantially higher risks from contaminated drinking water than their urban counterparts.

For example, [emphasis: in Fiji, 56 percent of rural residents use water sources classified as high or very high risk,] compared to 4 percent of urban residents. Many rural communities are geographically isolated and depend on untreated sources, such as surface water, rainwater, wells, and boreholes. Climate change and increasingly severe extreme weather events intensify the risk of microbial contamination.[reference: fiji1] There is a similar picture[emphasis: ]in [emphasis: Togo ]and the[emphasis: Republic of the Congo,] where 72 percent and 56 percent of rural residents use high-risk water sources, compared to 26 percent and 10 percent of urban residents, respectively.

content.title: Exposure to E. coli contamination is higher among rural residents than urban residents
content.subtitle: The 10 countries with the largest urban–rural gap in exposure to high or very high contamination risk at the point of collection (% of population)
content.source: Multiple Indicator Cluster Survey (MICS) program. MICS Surveys. UNICEF; Drinking Water Quality in Ethiopia Results from the 2016 Ethiopia Socioeconomic Survey, Central Statistical Agency of Ethiopia; 2017.
content.note: The data are compiled from country-level survey reports, including MICS and the Ethiopian Socioeconomic Survey.
content.visdescription: Bar chart showing selected countries with the largest shares of population exposed to high or very high E. coli contamination risk in drinking water. The chart allows comparison of contamination risk between urban (top bar) and rural (bottom bar) populations.

content.text: Chemical contaminants in water

Beyond risks from fecal contamination, [emphasis: chemical contaminants such as arsenic, fluoride, nitrates, and heavy metals also pose serious long-term health threats]. Among these, arsenic and fluoride are considered as priority concerns for drinking water.[reference: who_priority] At low concentrations, fluoride strengthens teeth; however, excessive exposure can lead to adverse effects on skeletal tissues, including dental and skeletal fluorosis,[reference: who_guideline] characterized by changes in bone and tooth structure. Arsenic contamination is likewise harmful, with prolonged exposure[reference: arsenic_exposure] associated with skin lesions, cancers, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive impairment.
Drinking water contaminated with arsenic in Bangladesh

Arsenic is a naturally occurring element found in the earth’s crust.  About 140 million people in at least 70 countries consume water containing arsenic at levels above WHO’s guideline value of 10 parts per billion (ppb).[reference: who_guideline]  [emphasis: The largest numbers of people exposed to arsenic in drinking water are in][emphasis:  Bangladesh and India].[reference: arsenic_exposure] [reference: arsenic_bgd] Bangladesh faces the world’s most severe arsenic crisis: since the 1990s, millions have been exposed, leading to cancers, skin disease, and adverse pregnancy outcomes.
In Bangladesh, around 17 percent of the population drinks water with arsenic concentrations above the WHO guideline.

content.title: Rural dwellers and poorer people  are more exposed to arsenic-contaminated water
content.subtitle: Arsenic contamination levels, by residence and wealth quintile (% of population)
content.source: Bangladesh Bureau of Statistics, UNICEF Bangladesh. Bangladesh MICS 2019 Water Quality Thematic Report. Dhaka, Bangladesh: 2021.
content.visdescription: These three bar charts show the share of the population in Bangladesh exposed to arsenic contamination in drinking water, grouped by concentration levels of 10–50 ppb and above 50 ppb. The left chart shows rural populations, the middle chart shows urban populations, and the right chart shows the total population.  In the next scene, the same data are presented by wealth quintile, with the poorest quintile on the left, the middle quintile in the center, and the richest quintile on the right.

scenes.text: Rural dwellers are more exposed to arsenic-contaminated water than urban dwellers. While 12 percent of the people in rural areas are exposed to arsenic levels exceeding 50 ppb, only 5 percent of the people in urban areas face the same challenge.
When we look at the arsenic contamination by wealth quintiles, people in the poorer and middle wealth quintiles face greater exposure than the wealthiest quintile.

content.text: [emphasis: Arsenic exposure is particularly high among users of wells, including tubewells, which are the most common source of drinking water in Bangladesh. ]Tubewells are a relatively low cost way to access shallow groundwater, which is generally less prone to bacterial contamination than surface water. However, in Bangladesh, due to geological conditions, many of these sources of water, located just a few meters below the ground, are contaminated with arsenic.[reference: arsenic_bgd]

Among people who rely on wells, roughly one in five (about 20 percent) are exposed to arsenic concentrations above the WHO guideline of 10 ppb. This risk is substantially lower for users of other water sources: only about 5 percent of those with piped water and 1.5 percent of those using alternative sources exceed this level. At higher concentrations, above the national standard of 50 ppb, the same pattern holds. About 12 percent of well users are exposed at these levels, compared with 3 percent of piped-water users and just 0.5 percent of those relying on other sources.

content.title: Arsenic contamination levels are highest in wells - including tubewells - in Bangladesh.
content.subtitle: Population exposure to arsenic contamination, by water source (% of population)
content.source: Bangladesh Bureau of Statistics, UNICEF Bangladesh. Bangladesh MICS 2019 Water Quality Thematic Report. Dhaka, Bangladesh: 2021.
content.note: Tubewells are narrow, drilled groundwater wells fitted with a pump to draw water from underground aquifers.
content.visdescription: The next three charts present the proportion of people exposed to arsenic-contaminated drinking water by source: water wells at the top, piped water in the middle, and other sources at the bottom. Each chart represents the population using icons (one person equals 1 percent), showing exposure levels of above 10  ppb and above 50 ppb.

content.text: The government’s initiatives and programs to solve arsenic issues

Since the 1990s, the Government of Bangladesh has introduced a wide range of programs to address arsenic contamination in drinking water. These efforts have included nationwide screening of tubewells, the installation of arsenic-safe water sources such as piped systems drawing on safe surface or deep groundwater, and the use of household and community filtration technologies. Public awareness campaigns have also been carried out, alongside health sector initiatives to identify and manage arsenic-related illnesses.

Despite these initiatives, arsenic contamination remains a pressing challenge. Millions of people continue to rely on unsafe water because arsenic-free options—such as deep wells, piped systems, or rainwater harvesting—are often unavailable, unaffordable, or too distant.[reference: arsenic_bgd] Research indicates that limited resources, gaps in local information, and the sheer number of people and wells affected further complicate mitigation efforts.[reference: arsenic_research] Countries like Bangladesh face the dual burden of microbial risks and arsenic contamination, a problem that is particularly difficult for low- and middle-income countries with constrained resources.
Why Water Quality Matters: Challenges and the Way Forward

Major challenges remain in ensuring water quality for all. While global access has progressed over the past two decades, deep inequalities persist across regions, countries, income groups, and between rural and urban areas. A major challenge is that in many low- and middle-income countries, drinking water is still contaminated by E. coli or harmful chemicals such as arsenic.

At the same time, strengthening sanitation and hygiene systems is critical, but sanitation programs, in particular, remain chronically underfunded in many countries.[reference: SWS]  Without adequate sanitation and hand hygiene, investments in water supply are undermined by contamination, perpetuating cycles of disease.

As Nobel laureate Michael Kremer emphasizes, the link between water quality, access to safe drinking water, and child survival is especially strong in low- and middle-income countries. Evidence shows that improved water treatment can reduce child mortality by 25 percent, with the greatest benefits for infants and young children.[reference: Kremer]
